en.extract:
The black-breasted buttonquail (Turnix melanogaster) is a rare buttonquail endemic to eastern Australia. As with other buttonquails, it is unrelated to the true quails. The black-breasted buttonquail is a plump quail-shaped bird 17–19 cm (6.7–7.5 in) in length with predominantly marbled black, rufous, and pale brown plumage, marked prominently with white spots and stripes, and white eyes. Like other buttonquails, the female is larger and more boldly coloured than the male, with a distinctive black head and neck sprinkled with fine white markings. The usual sex roles are reversed, as the female mates with multiple male partners and leaves them to incubate the eggs.
The black-breasted buttonquail is usually found in rainforests, foraging on the ground for invertebrates in large areas of thick leaf litter. Most of its original habitat has been cleared and the remaining populations are fragmented. The species is rated as vulnerable on the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN)'s Red List of Endangered species and is listed as vulnerable under the Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act 1999. A three-year conservation project has been under way since 2021.


Taxonomy
The black-breasted buttonquail was originally described by ornithologist John Gould in 1837 as Hemipodius melanogaster, from specimens collected around Moreton Bay in Queensland. Its specific epithet is derived from the Ancient Greek terms melas "black" and gaster "belly". In 1840 English zoologist George Robert Gray established that the genus name Turnix, coined in 1790 by French naturalist Pierre Joseph Bonnaterre, had priority over Hemipodius, which had been published in 1815 by Coenraad Jacob Temminck. In his 1865 Handbook to the Birds of Australia, Gould used its current name Turnix melanogaster. Gregory Mathews placed it in its own genus Colcloughia in 1913,  which was not followed by later authors. He also described a subspecies Colcloughia melanogaster goweri from Gowrie on the basis of less extensive black plumage, though this was later regarded as individual variation. Along with other buttonquails, the black-breasted buttonquail was traditionally placed in the order Gruiformes, but more recent molecular analysis shows it belongs to an early offshoot within the shorebirds (Charadriiformes).
"Black-breasted buttonquail" has been designated the official name by the International Ornithologists' Union (IOU). "Black-fronted buttonquail" is an alternative vernacular name. Gould called it "black-breasted hemipode" initially, and then "black-breasted turnix", corresponding with its scientific name. The buttonquail species were generally known as "quail" (hence  "black-breasted quail" or "black-fronted quail") until the Royal Australasian Ornithologists Union (RAOU) promoted the current usage of "buttonquail" in 1978, which was then universally adopted. The Butchulla people, the traditional owners of K'gari (Fraser Island), know the bird as the mur'rindum bird.


Description
The black-breasted buttonquail is a plump quail-shaped bird with predominantly marbled black, rufous and pale brown plumage, marked prominently with white spots and stripes, white eyes, a grey bill and yellowish feet. The short tail has twelve rectrices and the wings are short with round tips. The length ranges from 17 to 19 cm (6.5 to 7.5 in), with females tending to be larger and heavier, weighing 80–119 g (2.8–4.2 oz), compared to males, which weigh 50–87 g (1.8–3.1 oz). Like other buttonquails, the female is more distinctively coloured than the male. Its head, neck, and breast are black with a chestnut tinge on the nape and rear of its crown, and small white spots on its neck and face forming a moustache and eyebrow-like pattern. The white spots coalesce into bars on its breast, and its underparts are dark grey. The male has a whitish face and neck with black speckles and darker ear coverts, and a brown-grey crown and nape. Its breast has black and white bars and spots, with red-brown on its flanks and more grey with dark barring on the rest of its underparts. The juvenile resembles the adult male though has a blue-grey iris, duller brown-grey upperparts more heavily blotched with black on outer back and scapulars and less pale streaks.
The female makes a low-pitched oom call – a sequence of 5–7 notes that last 1.5–2.0 seconds each – which can be repeated 14–21 (or less commonly 1–4) times. This advertising call cannot be heard more than 50 m (160 ft) away, and is uttered only after there has been sufficient rainfall of 100 mm (4 in) within a few days. The female whistles quietly to its young. The male makes a range of high staccato and clucking alarm or rallying calls, including an ak ak call when separated from others in its covey. Juveniles have a range of chirping or piping calls to induce feeding or raise an alarm.
The black markings and large size of the female and the dark markings and whitish face of the male distinguish the species from the co-occurring painted buttonquail (Turnix varius). The regurgitated globular pellets of the black-breasted buttonquail have a distinctive hook at the end, in contrast to those of the painted buttonquail, which are more cylindrical and gently curved.


Distribution and habitat
The black-breasted buttonquail is found from Hervey Bay in central Queensland south to the northeastern corner of New South Wales, generally in areas receiving 770–1,200 mm (30–47 in) rainfall annually. There had been only ten reports from New South Wales in the decade leading up to 2009. Fieldwork across the Wide Bay–Burnett region from 2016 to 2018 found it in scattered locations in its suitable habitat from Teewah Beach to Inskip Point on the mainland and along the east coast of near K'Gari. It is found in Palmgrove National Park, which has been identified by BirdLife International as an Important Bird Area for the species. The black-breasted buttonquail was once populous on Inskip Point, with the area a destination for birdwatchers wanting to see this species.  Mike West, former president of Birds Queensland, blamed dingoes and wild dogs for wiping out the population.
The bird is rare and its habitat is fragmented. It is found in dry rainforest and nearby areas, as well as bottle tree (Brachychiton rupestris) scrub, lantana thickets, dune scrub, and mature hoop pine (Araucaria cunninghamii) plantations with a closed canopy and developed undergrowth. Many canopy plants such as Acacia species produce abundant leaf litter, which the species forages in. No other buttonquail species lives in its type of habitat.


Behaviour

The black-breasted buttonquail is generally ground-dwelling. It has no hind toe and so cannot perch in trees. If startled, it generally freezes or runs rather than flying.


Breeding
The usual sex roles are reversed in the buttonquail genus (Turnix), as the larger and more brightly-coloured female mates with multiple male partners and leaves them to incubate the eggs. The breeding habits of the species are not well known as both the birds and their nests are difficult to find and monitor. There are conflicting reports on the duration of the breeding season; field observations by John Young in northern New South Wales indicate this is restricted to between October and March, yet there are other reports of chicks year-round, suggesting opportune breeding can take place at any time. Minimum temperatures in the studied areas in New South Wales can drop to −2 °C (28 °F) in cooler months; reproduction has been known to be inhibited by cold weather in captivity, hence breeding may be related to temperature in this part of its range. For most of the year, the female black-breasted buttonquail forms a covey with one to three males. During breeding season, the female establishes a territory while the males often form small territories within it. Agonistic behaviour between females has been observed but it is unclear how common it is. The female utters drumming calls as courtship, which is answered by clucking from the male.
The nest is a shallow depression measuring  10 by 6 cm (4 by 2.5 in) scraped out of the leaf litter and ground, lined with leaves, moss and dried vegetation. It is often sited between the buttress roots of a plant, or in a crevice or sheltered by a tree root, and within or near undergrowth vegetation such as lantana (Lantana camara), bracken (Pteridium esculentum) or prickly rasp fern (Doodia aspera). It is not known which sex builds the nest. Three or four shiny grey-white or buff eggs splotched with dark brown-black and lavender are laid measuring 28 by 23 mm (1.10 by 0.91 in). Incubation lasts 18 to 21 days. The hatchlings are precocial and nidifugous, and are able to forage by 8–11 days of age, though parents may feed them for two weeks. By 8–12 weeks, they gain adult plumage and are able to breed at three to five months old.


Feeding
The black-breasted buttonquail forages on the ground in large areas of thick leaf litter in vine forest, and thickets of vines or lantana. Leaves fall on these areas year-round, with litter layers 3–10 cm (1–4 in) thick being preferred. A covey of birds scrapes out up to a hundred plate-shaped shallow feeding sites, though ten to forty is more usual. The buttonquail makes these by scratching at the ground with alternate legs in a circular pattern moving either clockwise or counterclockwise, creating the 20 cm (8 in) depression and pecking for invertebrates in the exposed ground. A 1995 study recovered the exoskeletons of ants, beetles (including weevils), spiders such as jumping spiders and the brown trapdoor spider (Euoplos variabilis), centipedes, millipedes, and snails such as Nitor pudibunda  from pellets; the remains of soft-bodied invertebrates were not discernible. A 2018 analysis of faecal pellets revealed beetles, ants and earwigs to be prominent, though the authors concluded the black-breasted buttonquail is a generalist insectivore. Plant material was scant, though this might have been an artefact due to its greater digestibility.


Conservation status
The species is classified as vulnerable on the International Union for Conservation of Nature's Red List. It is listed as vulnerable by the Australian Government under the Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act 1999. On a state level, it is listed as 'Vulnerable' under the Queensland Nature Conservation Act 1992 and 'Endangered' under the New South Wales Threatened Species Conservation Act 1995.
The population has been estimated at as few as 2500 breeding birds and declining, with no single population containing more than 250 individuals. The dry rainforest it lives in, although often adjacent to wet rainforest, is often located outside of national parks and protected areas and is thus at risk from further clearance for agriculture or development. Since European settlement, 90% of its habitat has been lost and much of what is left is fragmented. Furthermore, fieldwork in southeast Queensland showed that it did not forage in remnants under 7 ha (17 acres) in area. On the mainland, they are also at risk from feral animals such as cats, foxes and pigs, as well as humans, and weeds.
As of 2021, the Butchulla Land and Sea Rangers are collaborating with researchers on a three-year project aimed at reducing threats to the bird and improving its habitat to ensure survival into the future. In August 2021 they set up 19 cameras on K'gari and five at Inskip Point and Double Island Point, leaving them in place for seven weeks. They saw evidence of damage from feral animals on the mainland, but also saw baby birds, and much evidence of the birds at Rainbow Beach and Inskip Point on the mainland, and Dilli Village and Champagne Pools on the island. They are setting pig and cat traps and managing weeds in the area, and will be doing traditional burns in the winter to manage the risk of bushfire on the island.


References


External links
Explore Species: Black-breasted Buttonquail at eBird (Cornell Lab of Ornithology)

The '''black-breasted buttonquail''' ('''''Turnix melanogaster''''') is a rare [[buttonquail]] endemic to eastern Australia. As with other buttonquails, it is unrelated to the true [[quail]]s. The black-breasted buttonquail is a plump quail-shaped bird {{cvt|17–19|cm}} in length with predominantly marbled black, rufous, and pale brown plumage, marked prominently with white spots and stripes, and white eyes. Like other buttonquails, the female is larger and more boldly coloured than the male, with a distinctive black head and neck sprinkled with fine white markings. The usual sex roles are reversed, as the female mates with [[Polyandry|multiple male]] partners and leaves them to [[Avian incubation|incubate]] the eggs.

The black-breasted buttonquail is usually found in [[rainforest]]s, foraging on the ground for invertebrates in large areas of thick [[leaf litter]]. Most of its original habitat has been cleared and the remaining populations are [[Habitat fragmentation|fragment]]ed. The species is rated as vulnerable on the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] (IUCN)'s [[IUCN Red List|Red List]] of Endangered species and is listed as [[Vulnerable species|vulnerable]] under the ''[[Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act 1999]]''. A three-year conservation project has been under way since 2021.

==Taxonomy==
The black-breasted buttonquail was originally [[Species description|describe]]d by ornithologist [[John Gould]] in 1837 as ''Hemipodius melanogaster'',<ref>{{cite journal | last=Gould | first= John | year=1837 | volume = 5 | pages=7–8  | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30570892| title= In Proceedings of meeting of Zoological Society of London | journal=Proceedings of Meeting of Zoological Society of London}}</ref> from specimens collected around [[Moreton Bay]] in [[Queensland]].<ref name="AFD"/> Its [[specific epithet]] is derived from the [[Ancient Greek]] terms {{Lang|grc-Latn|melas}} "black" and {{Lang|grc-Latn|gaster}} "belly".<ref>{{cite book | last= Jobling | first= James A | year= 2010| title= The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | url= https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling |publisher = Christopher Helm | isbn = 978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=[https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/61536485 247] }}</ref> In 1840 English zoologist [[George Robert Gray]] established that the genus name ''Turnix'', coined in 1790 by French naturalist [[Pierre Joseph Bonnaterre]], had priority over ''Hemipodius'', which had been published in 1815 by [[Coenraad Jacob Temminck]].<ref>{{ cite book | last=Gray | first=George Robert | author-link=George Robert Gray | year=1840 | title=A List of the Genera of Birds: with an indication of the typical species of each genus | publisher=R. and J.E. Taylor | page=81 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14050312}}</ref> In his 1865 ''[[Handbook to the Birds of Australia]]'', Gould used its current name ''Turnix melanogaster''.<ref name=gould1865>{{cite book|last=Gould|first=John |title=Handbook to the Birds of Australia|publisher=Self-published| year=1865|volume=2 | page=178 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/13966203}}</ref> [[Gregory Mathews]] placed it in its own genus ''Colcloughia'' in 1913,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Mathews |first1=Gregory M. |title=New Genera |journal=Austral Avian Records |date=1913 |volume=2 |issue=5 |pages=110–112|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/34804718}}</ref>  which was not followed by later authors.<ref name="AFD"/> He also described a subspecies ''Colcloughia melanogaster goweri'' from [[Gowrie Junction, Queensland|Gowrie]] on the basis of less extensive black plumage,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Mathews |first1=Gregory M. |title=List of additions of new sub-species to, and changes in, my "List of the Birds of Australia" |journal=Austral Avian Records |date=1916 |volume=3 |issue=3 |pages=53–58 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/33271252}}</ref> though this was later regarded as individual variation.<ref name="McGowan 2010"/> Along with other buttonquails, the black-breasted buttonquail was traditionally placed in the order [[Gruiformes]], but more recent molecular analysis shows it belongs to an [[Basal (phylogenetics)|early offshoot]] within the shorebirds ([[Charadriiform]]es).<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Paton |first1=Tara A. |last2=Baker |first2=Allan J. |last3=Groth |first3=Jeff G. |last4=Barrowclough |first4=George F. |year=2003|title=RAG-1 sequences resolve phylogenetic relationships within charadriiform birds |journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |volume=29 |pages=268–278 |doi=10.1016/S1055-7903(03)00098-8 |pmid=13678682 |issue=2 |bibcode=2003MolPE..29..268P }}</ref>

"Black-breasted buttonquail" has been designated the official name by the [[International Ornithologists' Union]] (IOU).<ref name="ioclist">{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | year=2021| title= Buttonquail, thick-knees, sheathbills, plovers, oystercatchers, stilts, painted-snipes, jacanas, Plains-wanderer, seedsnipes | work=World Bird List Version 12.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/buttonquail/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=9 April 2022 }}</ref> "Black-fronted buttonquail" is an alternative vernacular name.<ref name="McGowan 2010"/> Gould called it "black-breasted hemipode" initially,<ref>{{cite book | last=Gould | first= John | year=1848 | volume = 5 | pages=81–82  | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48401255 | title= The Birds of Australia | publisher= Self-published}}</ref> and then "black-breasted turnix", corresponding with its scientific name.<ref name=gould1865/> The buttonquail species were generally known as "quail" (hence  "black-breasted quail" or "black-fronted quail") until the [[Royal Australasian Ornithologists Union]] (RAOU) promoted the current usage of "buttonquail" in 1978, which was then universally adopted.<ref name="fraser13">{{cite book |last1=Gray |first1=Jeannie |last2=Fraser |first2=Ian |title=Australian Bird Names: A Complete Guide |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W1TCqHVWQp0C&pg=PT111 |year=2013 |publisher=Csiro Publishing |isbn=978-0-643-10471-6 |page=111}}</ref> The [[Butchulla people]], the [[traditional owner]]s of [[K'gari]] (Fraser Island), know the bird as the ''mur'rindum'' bird.<ref name=abc2021/>

==Description==
The black-breasted buttonquail is a plump [[quail]]-shaped bird with predominantly marbled black, rufous and pale brown plumage, marked prominently with white spots and stripes, white eyes,<ref name=Slater70>{{cite book |title=A Field Guide to Australian Birds:Non-passerines |last=Slater |first=Peter|year=1970 |publisher=Rigby  |isbn=0-85179-102-6|page=264}}</ref> a grey bill and yellowish feet. The short tail has twelve [[rectrice]]s and the wings are short with round tips. The length ranges from {{cvt|17|to|19|cm|in|round=0.5}}, with females tending to be larger and heavier, weighing {{cvt|80–119|g|oz}}, compared to males, which weigh {{cvt|50–87|g|oz}}. Like other buttonquails, the female is more distinctively coloured than the male. Its head, neck, and breast are black with a chestnut tinge on the {{Birdgloss|mantle|nape}} and rear of its {{Birdgloss|crown}}, and small white spots on its neck and face forming a moustache and eyebrow-like pattern. The white spots coalesce into bars on its breast, and its {{Birdgloss|underparts}} are dark grey. The male has a whitish face and neck with black speckles and darker {{Birdgloss|ear coverts}}, and a brown-grey crown and nape. Its breast has black and white bars and spots, with red-brown on its flanks and more grey with dark barring on the rest of its underparts.<ref name="McGowan 2010"/> The juvenile resembles the adult male though has a blue-grey iris, duller brown-grey {{Birdgloss|upperparts}} more heavily blotched with black on outer back and {{Birdgloss|scapulars}} and less pale streaks.<ref name="McGowan 2010"/>

The female makes a low-pitched ''oom'' call<ref name=Slater70/>{{snd}}a sequence of 5–7 notes that last 1.5–2.0 seconds each{{snd}}which can be repeated 14–21 (or less commonly 1–4) times. This advertising call cannot be heard more than {{cvt|50|m|ft}} away, and is uttered only after there has been sufficient rainfall of {{cvt|100|mm|in|0}} within a few days. The female whistles quietly to its young.<ref name="McGowan 2010"/> The male makes a range of high staccato and clucking alarm or rallying calls,<ref name="McGowan 2010"/> including an ''ak ak'' call when separated from others in its covey.<ref name="Mcconnell95"/> Juveniles have a range of chirping or piping calls to induce feeding or raise an alarm.<ref name="McGowan 2010"/>

The black markings and large size of the female and the dark markings and whitish face of the male distinguish the species from the co-occurring [[painted buttonquail]] (''Turnix varius'').<ref name="McGowan 2010"/> The regurgitated globular [[Pellet (ornithology)|pellet]]s of the black-breasted buttonquail have a distinctive hook at the end, in contrast to those of the painted buttonquail, which are more cylindrical and gently curved.<ref name="Mcconnell95"/>

==Distribution and habitat==
The black-breasted buttonquail is found from [[Hervey Bay]] in central [[Queensland]] south to the northeastern corner of [[New South Wales]],<ref name = "Curtis">{{cite book | title = Queensland's Threatened Animals | editor-first = Lee K. | editor-last = Curtis | editor2-first = Andrew J. | editor2-last = Dennis | editor3-first = Keith R. | editor3-last = McDonald | year = 2012 | publisher = CSIRO Publishing | page = 276 | isbn = 978-0-643-09614-1 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=gsaXOp6qKawC&pg=PA276}}</ref> generally in areas receiving {{cvt|770|–|1200|mm|in}} rainfall annually.<ref name="McGowan 2010"/> There had been only ten reports from New South Wales in the decade leading up to 2009.<ref name=env>{{cite web |last1=Department of Environment and Resource Management |title=National recovery plan for the black-breasted button-quail (''Turnix melanogaster'') |url=https://www.awe.gov.au/environment/biodiversity/threatened/recovery-plans/black-breasted-button-quail-turnix-melanogaster-2009 |website=Biodiversity |publisher=Department of Agriculture, Water and the Environment |access-date=14 April 2022 | date=3 October 2021}}</ref> Fieldwork across the [[Wide Bay–Burnett]] region from 2016 to 2018 found it in scattered locations in its suitable habitat from [[Teewah Beach]] to [[Inskip Point]] on the mainland and along the east coast of near K'Gari.<ref name=webster2022>{{cite journal | last1=Webster | first1= Patrick T.D.  | first2= Reisuke | last2=Shimomura| first3= Emily R. | last3=Rush| first4= Luke K-P. | last4=Leung| first5= Peter J. | last5=Murray | title=Distribution of Black-breasted Button-quail ''Turnix melanogaster'' in the Great Sandy Region, Queensland and associations with vegetation communities | journal= Emu – Austral Ornithology | doi=10.1080/01584197.2022.2047733 | date= March 2022| volume= 122 | issue= 1 | pages= 71–76| bibcode= 2022EmuAO.122...71W | s2cid= 247534104 }}</ref> It is found in [[Palmgrove National Park]], which has been identified by [[BirdLife International]] as an [[Important Bird Area]] for the species.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/site/factsheet/palmgrove-iba-australia |title= Important Bird Areas factsheet: Palmgrove |access-date=6 May 2022 | date=2022 |work=Birdata |publisher=Birds Australia}}</ref> The black-breasted buttonquail was once populous on Inskip Point, with the area a destination for birdwatchers wanting to see this species.  Mike West, former president of Birds Queensland, blamed [[dingo]]es and wild dogs for wiping out the population.<ref name="Dingo">{{cite news |url=http://www.frasercoastchronicle.com.au/news/dingoes-are-blamed-for-quail-tragedy/1764050/ |title=Dingoes and wild dogs blamed for quail tragedy |last1=Johnson |first1=Hayden |date=21 February 2013 |newspaper=Fraser Coast Chronicle |access-date=23 February 2013}}</ref><!-- cites previous three sentences -->

The bird is rare and its habitat is [[Habitat fragmentation|fragmented]]. It is found in [[Rainforest#Dry rainforest|dry rainforest]] and nearby areas,<ref name=smyth01/> as well as bottle tree (''[[Brachychiton rupestris]]'') [[Shrubland|scrub]],<ref name="McGowan 2010">{{cite book|last=McGowan|first=Phil|author2=Madge, Steve|title=Pheasants, Partridges & Grouse: Including buttonquails, sandgrouse and allies|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|date=2010|orig-date=2002|pages=429–430|isbn=978-1-4081-3565-5|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WffUBAAAQBAJ&dq=brachychiton+rupestris&pg=PA429}}</ref> [[lantana]] thickets,<ref name="McGowan 2010"/> [[Sand dune|dune]] scrub,<ref name=abc2021/> and mature hoop pine (''[[Araucaria cunninghamii]]'') plantations with a closed [[Canopy (biology)|canopy]] and developed [[undergrowth]].<ref>{{Cite journal |doi = 10.1080/00049158.1999.10674800|title = Use of mature hoop pine plantation by the vulnerable Black-breasted Button-quail (''Turnix melanogaster'')|journal = Australian Forestry|volume = 62|issue = 4|pages = 330–335|year = 1999|last1 = Lees|first1 = Nadya|last2 = Smith|first2 = Geoffrey C. | bibcode=1999AuFor..62..330L }}</ref> Many canopy plants such as ''[[Acacia]]'' species produce abundant [[leaf litter]], which the species forages in.<ref name=webster2022/> No other buttonquail species lives in its type of habitat.<ref name=abc2021/>

==Behaviour==
[[File:Black-breasted buttonquail (Turnix melanogaster) by John Gould.jpg|thumb|alt=drawings of two bird heads|Illustrations of male (above) and female (below) by John Gould]]

The black-breasted buttonquail is generally ground-dwelling.<ref name="McGowan 2010"/> It has no hind toe and so cannot perch in trees.<ref name=abc2021/> If startled, it generally freezes or runs rather than flying.<ref name="McGowan 2010"/>

===Breeding===
The usual sex roles are reversed in the buttonquail genus (''[[Turnix]]''), as the larger and more brightly-coloured female mates with [[Polyandry in nature|multiple male]] partners and leaves them to [[Avian incubation|incubate]] the eggs.<ref>{{cite book |title=Every Australian Bird Illustrated|editor=Wade Peter |year=1977 |pages=78–79|publisher=Rigby|isbn=0-7270-0009-8}}</ref> The breeding habits of the species are not well known as both the birds and their nests are difficult to find and monitor. There are conflicting reports on the duration of the breeding season; field observations by [[John Young (naturalist)|John Young]] in northern New South Wales indicate this is restricted to between October and March, yet there are other reports of chicks year-round, suggesting opportune breeding can take place at any time. Minimum temperatures in the studied areas in New South Wales can drop to {{cvt|−2|C|F}} in cooler months; reproduction has been known to be inhibited by cold weather in captivity, hence breeding may be related to temperature in this part of its range. For most of the year, the female black-breasted buttonquail forms a covey with one to three males. During breeding season, the female establishes a territory while the males often form small territories within it. Agonistic behaviour between females has been observed but it is unclear how common it is. The female utters drumming calls as courtship, which is answered by clucking from the male.<ref name=smyth96>{{Cite journal |doi = 10.1071/MU9960202|title = Observations on the Endangered Black-breasted Button-quail ''Turnix melanogaster'' breeding in the wild|journal = Emu – Austral Ornithology|volume = 96|issue = 3|pages = 202–207|year = 1996|last1 = Smyth|first1 = Anita K.|last2 = Young|first2 = John | bibcode=1996EmuAO..96..202S }}</ref><!-- cites previous four sentences -->

The nest is a shallow depression measuring  {{cvt|10|by|6|cm|in|round=0.5}} scraped out of the leaf litter and ground, lined with leaves, moss and dried vegetation. It is often sited between the [[buttress root]]s of a plant, or in a crevice or sheltered by a tree root, and within or near undergrowth vegetation such as lantana (''[[Lantana camara]]''), bracken (''[[Pteridium esculentum]]'') or prickly rasp fern (''[[Doodia aspera]]''). It is not known which sex builds the nest.<ref name=smyth96/> Three or four shiny grey-white or buff eggs splotched with dark brown-black and lavender are laid measuring {{cvt|28|by|23|mm|in}}.<ref name=Ber93>{{cite book  | last = Beruldsen  | first = Gordon  | title = Australian Birds: Their Nests and Eggs  | publisher = self  | year = 2003  | page = 212|isbn = 0-646-42798-9}}</ref> Incubation lasts 18 to 21 days.<ref name=smyth96/> The hatchlings are [[precocial]] and [[nidifugous]], and are able to forage by 8–11 days of age, though parents may feed them for two weeks. By 8–12 weeks, they gain adult plumage and are able to breed at three to five months old.<ref name="McGowan 2010"/>

===Feeding===
The black-breasted buttonquail forages on the ground in large areas of thick [[leaf litter]] in vine forest, and thickets of vines or lantana. Leaves fall on these areas year-round,<ref name=smyth01/> with litter layers {{cvt|3–10|cm|sigfig=1}} thick being preferred.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Reif |first1=Stephanie |title=Creating habitat for Black-breasted Button-quails |url=https://www.lfwseq.org.au/creating-habitat-black-breasted-button-quails/ |website=Land for Wildlife: South East Queensland |date=9 July 2017 |access-date=5 April 2022}}</ref> A covey of birds scrapes out up to a hundred plate-shaped shallow feeding sites, though ten to forty is more usual.<ref name=smyth01/> The buttonquail makes these by scratching at the ground with alternate legs in a circular pattern moving either clockwise or counterclockwise, creating the {{cvt|20|cm|sigfig=1}} depression and pecking for invertebrates in the exposed ground. A 1995 study recovered the exoskeletons of ants, beetles (including weevils), spiders such as jumping spiders and the brown trapdoor spider (''[[Euoplos variabilis]]''), centipedes, millipedes, and snails such as ''[[Nitor pudibunda]]''  from pellets; the remains of soft-bodied invertebrates were not discernible.<ref name="Mcconnell95">{{cite journal |last1=Mcconnell |first1=P.| last2=Hobson | first2=R. |title=The diet and behaviour of the Black-breasted Button-quail ''Turnix melanogaster'' |journal=Sunbird |date=1995 |volume=25 |issue=1 |pages=18–23| issn=1037-258X}}</ref> A 2018 analysis of faecal pellets revealed beetles, ants and earwigs to be prominent, though the authors concluded the black-breasted buttonquail is a generalist insectivore. Plant material was scant, though this might have been an artefact due to its greater digestibility.<ref>{{cite journal |last1= Webster | first1=Patrick | last2=Lisle  | first2= Allan | first3= Peter John | last3= Murray | title= Faecal analysis of the diet of Black-breasted Button-quail  | journal = Corella | volume=43 | pages=19–25 | year=2019}}</ref>

==Conservation status==
The species is classified as [[Vulnerable species|vulnerable]] on the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]]'s [[IUCN Red List|Red List]].<ref name=iucn/> It is listed as vulnerable by the Australian Government under the ''[[Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act 1999]]''.<ref name=abc2021>{{cite web | last1=Doole | first1=Stephanie |first2=David |last2=Dowsett| title=Traditional owners on mission to save vulnerable black-breasted button quail on K'gari Fraser Island  | website=ABC News| publisher= [[Australian Broadcasting Corporation]] | date=5 December 2021 | url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2021-12-06/saving-murrindum-quail-bird-on-kgari-fraser-island-butchulla/100673210 | access-date=27 December 2021 }}</ref> On a state level, it is listed as 'Vulnerable' under the Queensland ''[[Nature Conservation Act 1992]]'' and 'Endangered' under the New South Wales ''[[Threatened Species Conservation Act 1995]]''.<ref name=env/>

The population has been estimated at as few as 2500 breeding birds and declining, with no single population containing more than 250 individuals. The dry rainforest it lives in, although often adjacent to wet rainforest, is often located outside of national parks and protected areas and is thus at risk from further clearance for agriculture or development. Since European settlement, 90% of its habitat has been lost and much of what is left is fragmented. Furthermore, fieldwork in southeast Queensland showed that it did not forage in remnants under {{cvt|7|ha}} in area.<ref name=smyth01>{{Cite journal |doi = 10.1016/S0006-3207(00)00149-X|last1 = Smyth|first1 = Anita K |last2=Pavey | first2= Chris R.| year= 2001 | title= Foraging by the endangered black-breasted button-quail (''Turnix melanogaster'') within fragmented rainforest of an agricultural landscape | journal= Biological Conservation | volume=98 | issue=2 | pages= 149–157 | bibcode=2001BCons..98..149S }}</ref> On the mainland, they are also at risk from [[feral animal]]s such as [[Feral cats in Australia|cat]]s, [[Red foxes in Australia|fox]]es and [[Feral pig|pig]]s, as well as humans, and weeds.<ref name=abc2021/>

{{as of|2021}}, the [[Indigenous ranger|Butchulla Land and Sea Rangers]] are collaborating with researchers on a three-year project aimed at reducing threats to the bird and improving its habitat to ensure survival into the future. In August 2021 they set up 19 cameras on K'gari and five at Inskip Point and [[Double Island Point]], leaving them in place for seven weeks. They saw evidence of damage from feral animals on the mainland, but also saw baby birds, and much evidence of the birds at [[Rainbow Beach, Queensland|Rainbow Beach]] and Inskip Point on the mainland, and Dilli Village and Champagne Pools on the island. They are setting pig and cat traps and managing weeds in the area, and will be doing [[Firestick farming|traditional burn]]s in the winter to manage the risk of [[bushfire]] on the island.<ref name=abc2021/>
